description_long: This object is a solar energy infrastructure installation featuring multiple rectangular solar panels mounted on a flat, platform-like base. The panels are arranged in a staggered, partially tilted configuration, with some panels elevated at an angle and others lying closer to the base. The base structure appears to be constructed from a grid of metallic or composite materials, with raised sections and block-like supports distributed across its surface. The overall design suggests a modular, possibly urban rooftop or ground-mounted solar array, with the panels oriented to maximize sunlight exposure. The color palette is dominated by shades of black, gray, and blue, giving the installation a modern, industrial appearance. No visible cabling or inverter units are apparent, and the structure is robust enough to support additional weight or foot traffic.
description: A modular solar energy installation with multiple tilted and flat solar panels on a sturdy, grid-like base, featuring black, gray, and blue tones.
description_view_0: From this view, two main solar panels are visible in the foreground, both mounted on raised supports, with the grid-like base and additional blocky structures behind.
description_view_1: This side view highlights the varying tilt angles of the solar panels and the stepped, platform-like base, showing the staggered arrangement of the panels.
description_view_2: The rear view reveals the back sides of the solar panels and the blocky supports, with the base's grid pattern and additional raised sections more prominent.
description_view_3: This opposite side view emphasizes the angular placement of the panels and the distribution of support structures across the base.
attribute_panel_orientation: Panels are tilted at approximately 30-40 degrees, with some flat; azimuth direction not clearly defined; orientation appears fixed.
attribute_mounting_structure_type: ground-mounted with modular block supports, likely aluminum or steel frame.
attribute_array_configuration: Multiple panels arranged in a staggered, non-uniform cluster, approximately 4-6 panels in total.
